access.entityLabel: British Library Cotton MS Nero A X/2
notes: The current catalog explicitly reports the digital images as unavailable.

entities.description: The unique manuscript witness of Sir Gawain and the Green Knight and of Pearl, Cleanness, and Patience. Gawain occupies modern folios 94v–130r; the older foliation embedded in the repository XML runs 91r–124v.

historical_designations: Cotton MS Nero A X, article 3
Cotton MS Nero A X, ff. 41–130
holding.notes: The original composite Cotton MS Nero A X was separated into two volumes in 1964.

entities.label: British Library Cotton MS Nero A X/2
notes: Do not describe the repository XML's folio milestones as modern BL foliation.
The witness date is separate from the poem's composition date and the later illustration date.

physicalDescription.extent: 90 folios in the separately bound volume; Gawain at modern folios 94v–130r
physicalDescription.format: Parchment codex, written in one column of 36 lines
notes: Gothic cursive script described as textura rotunda with anglicana features.
The manuscript has two foliation sequences; the BL catalog uses modern pencil foliation, while the XML milestones preserve the older sequence.
The text was copied by one scribe in a South-East Cheshire or North-East Staffordshire dialect; the poet's dialect is distinguished as farther south in Staffordshire.
The BL record lists three Gawain illustrations at modern folios 94v, 129r–v, and 130r, with the first and last leaves carrying multiple scenes.

rights.basis: The text is attested in a manuscript copied between 1375 and 1400 and is centuries beyond any U.S. copyright term.
rights.component: The anonymous medieval textual content of Sir Gawain and the Green Knight
notes: This concerns the old text, not modern photographs, scans, transcriptions, editions, catalog prose, or provider contracts.

lineage.summary: The checked-in Gawain XML is a local repository representation of a University of Michigan/Oxford Text Archive electronic text distributed in 1993. Its declared immediate source is the 1967 second edition of J. R. R. Tolkien and E. V. Gordon's text, revised by Norman Davis; that edition revises the 1925 first edition and ultimately represents the unique medieval witness, British Library Cotton MS Nero A X/2. The manuscript and print editions, the electronic encoding, and the repository artifact are modeled as distinct entities so that dates, foliation, access, and rights statements are not inherited across layers.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous (Gawain Poet)
catalogSummary.editor: J. R. R. Tolkien and E. V. Gordon; revised by Norman Davis
catalogSummary.region: Staffordshire, England (poet's dialect); unique witness copied in the North-West Midlands
catalogSummary.title: Sir Gawain and the Green Knight

catalogingSubject.label: Sir Gawain and the Green Knight
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous late-fourteenth-century Middle English poem, not any particular manuscript, scholarly edition, digital encoding, translation, or modern adaptation.

contentParts.label: Passus I
contentParts.summary: Arthur's New Year feast, the Green Knight's beheading challenge, Gawain's acceptance and blow, and the covenant to receive a return stroke after a year and a day.

contentParts.label: Passus II
contentParts.summary: Gawain arms and departs, crosses the wintry wilderness, reaches Hautdesert for Christmas, and agrees to exchange his gains with the lord of the castle.

contentParts.label: Passus III
contentParts.summary: Three hunts alternate with three bedroom temptations. Gawain returns kisses under the exchange pact but accepts and conceals the lady's green girdle on the third day.

contentParts.label: Passus IV
contentParts.summary: At the Green Chapel the third feigned blow nicks Gawain. The Green Knight reveals the linked tests, Gawain confesses his fault, and Camelot adopts the green girdle when he returns.

assertion.notes: This is a scholarly work-level dating, not the date 1400 supplied in the legacy XML profile header and not the full catalogue date range for the physical codex.

notes: The copying date is evidence for attestation and a terminus for composition; it is not automatically the composition date.

extent.description: The repository XML contains four passus, 100 LG verse-group elements, and 2,533 L elements. The encoded group numbering runs 1–101 but omits a separate LG numbered 14, and XML line elements do not map one-for-one to the scholarly database's 2,530-line count; these are encoding metrics, not a silent emendation of the poem.

formStatements.description: Alliterative long-line verse arranged in variable-length stanza groups ending in a rhymed bob and wheel.

languageStatements.label: Anglo-Norman French
notes: The motto is separately attested in the encoded text; it does not make French a co-primary language of the poem.

scope.description: The closing motto HONY SOYT QUI MAL PENCE

notes: The legacy XML profile date 1400 is retained as lineage evidence but is not treated as an exact composition date.
The catalog distinguishes the poem, the sole manuscript witness, the 1967 source edition, the U-M/OTA digital encoding, and the checked-in repository artifact.
The XML's older foliation and the BL catalogue's modern foliation are both recorded in lineage; neither has been silently converted into the other.

openQuestions.question: Can the poem's composition be dated more narrowly than the late fourteenth century without conflating composition with copying or illustration dates?

next_steps: Review current authorship studies and distinguish shared scribal transmission from authorial attribution.
Retain Anonymous as the responsibility unless a stronger consensus statement is documented.
openQuestions.question: What confidence should the catalog assign to common authorship of Gawain, Pearl, Cleanness, and Patience?

assertion.notes: The British Library distinguishes the poet's dialect, thought to lie farther south in Staffordshire, from the unique witness's scribal dialect.

notes: Dialect localization does not prove an exact place of composition or the author's residence.
This statement localizes the language of the sole surviving copy, not necessarily the author's original spelling system.
placeStatements.placeLabel: South-East Cheshire or North-East Staffordshire, England

assertion.notes: The conventional label Gawain Poet identifies an unknown author; it is not a recovered personal name.

summaries.value: At Arthur's New Year feast, a supernatural green knight proposes a beheading exchange. Gawain accepts, strikes the first blow, and a year later journeys to keep the return appointment. At Hautdesert his truth is tested through an exchange-of-winnings pact and three bedroom temptations; he conceals a supposedly protective green girdle. The Green Knight reveals the tests at the Green Chapel, and Gawain returns to Camelot chastened by the gap between chivalric ideals and human conduct.

The poem survives uniquely in British Library Cotton MS Nero A X/2 with Pearl, Cleanness, and Patience. The repository text is not a diplomatic transcription of the manuscript: it descends through Tolkien and Gordon's edition as revised by Norman Davis and a U-M/OTA electronic encoding that omits the print edition's front and back matter, notes, and illustrations.

assertion.notes: Confirmed as the established preferred title; the unique manuscript does not supply this title as an authorial heading.

titles.value: Sir Gawain and the Green Knight
Siþen þe sege and þe assaut watz sesed at Troye

work.summary: At Arthur's New Year feast, a supernatural green knight proposes a beheading exchange. Gawain accepts, strikes the first blow, and a year later journeys to keep the return appointment. At Hautdesert his truth is tested through an exchange-of-winnings pact and three bedroom temptations; he conceals a supposedly protective green girdle. The Green Knight reveals the tests at the Green Chapel, and Gawain returns to Camelot chastened by the gap between chivalric ideals and human conduct.
